content.text: Publishing a managed application is the process of authoring, validating, packaging, uploading, and registering the application definition so it is available for consumers to deploy. Whether targeting a Service Catalog (internal) or Azure Marketplace (public), the publishing steps follow the same sequence — the difference is the final registration destination.

attrs.alt: Publishing a managed application end-to-end flow

content.text: The package requires exactly two files. Both must be at the root of the .zip archive (no nested directories).
The ARM template deployed into the managed resource group
Must be valid ARM JSON; Bicep compiles to this
Portal wizard UI definition for consumer input
Controls what parameters consumers can set and how inputs are validated
Before packaging, validate both files. ARM-TTK (ARM Template Test Toolkit) checks the template for common issues; the CreateUiDefinition Sandbox lets you preview the portal wizard without deploying.

# Install ARM TTK
git clone https://github.com/Azure/arm-ttk.git

# Run template tests
pwsh -Command "
  Import-Module ./arm-ttk/arm-ttk/arm-ttk.psd1
  Test-AzTemplate -TemplatePath ./mainTemplate.json
"

# Compile Bicep -> ARM JSON before running TTK
az bicep build --file main.bicep --outfile mainTemplate.json

# Preview the createUiDefinition in the Azure Portal Sandbox:
# https://portal.azure.com/#blade/Microsoft_Azure_CreateUIDef/SandboxBlade
# Upload createUiDefinition.json in the sandbox to test the wizard UI

# Validate the ARM template structure
az deployment group validate \
  --resource-group test-rg \
  --template-file mainTemplate.json \
  --parameters appName=testapp appServiceSku=B1

# Ensure both files are present
ls mainTemplate.json createUiDefinition.json

# Create the zip with files at the root (no parent directory)
zip -j managed-app-v1.0.0.zip mainTemplate.json createUiDefinition.json

# Verify the zip structure
unzip -l managed-app-v1.0.0.zip
# Expected output:
# Archive:  managed-app-v1.0.0.zip
#   Length      Date    Time    Name
# ---------  ---------- -----   ----
#      2048  2024-03-01 12:00   mainTemplate.json
#      1024  2024-03-01 12:00   createUiDefinition.json
# ---------                     -------
#      3072                     2 files
The -j flag in zip -j strips directory paths from file entries. Without it, the files would be nested under a directory inside the zip, which causes ARM to reject the package.

ACCOUNT=mytemplatesstorage
CONTAINER=managed-apps
VERSION=1.0.0
BLOB="managed-app-v${VERSION}.zip"

# Create the storage account and container if needed
az storage account create \
  --name $ACCOUNT --resource-group platform-rg \
  --sku Standard_LRS --kind StorageV2 \
  --min-tls-version TLS1_2

az storage container create \
  --account-name $ACCOUNT --name $CONTAINER

# Upload the package
az storage blob upload \
  --account-name $ACCOUNT \
  --container-name $CONTAINER \
  --name $BLOB \
  --file managed-app-v${VERSION}.zip

# Generate a SAS URI valid for 2 years
EXPIRY=$(date -u -d '+2 years' '+%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ' 2>/dev/null || \
         date -u -v +2y '+%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ')  # macOS fallback

SAS_URI=$(az storage blob generate-sas \
  --account-name $ACCOUNT \
  --container-name $CONTAINER \
  --name $BLOB \
  --permissions r \
  --expiry $EXPIRY \
  --full-uri -o tsv)

echo "Package URI: $SAS_URI"

# Get the AAD group that will manage deployed instances
MGMT_GROUP_ID=$(az ad group show --group 'PlatformOps' --query id -o tsv)
CONTRIBUTOR_ROLE='b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c'

# Publish the Service Catalog definition
az managedapp definition create \
  --name 'StandardWebApp' \
  --resource-group platform-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --lock-level ReadOnly \
  --display-name 'Standard Web Application v1.0' \
  --description 'App Service with org-standard config' \
  --authorizations "${MGMT_GROUP_ID}:${CONTRIBUTOR_ROLE}" \
  --package-file-uri "$SAS_URI" \
  --tags version=1.0.0 team=platform

# Verify the definition was created
az managedapp definition show \
  --name StandardWebApp \
  --resource-group platform-rg \
  --query '{id:id,lockLevel:properties.lockLevel,state:properties.provisioningState}'
Automating Publishing with a CI/CD Pipeline
The five publish steps can be combined into a GitHub Actions workflow that triggers on changes to the package source files.

name: Publish Managed App
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - 'managed-app/**'

env:
  STORAGE_ACCOUNT: mytemplatesstorage
  STORAGE_CONTAINER: managed-apps
  DEFINITION_RG: platform-rg
  DEFINITION_NAME: StandardWebApp

jobs:
  publish:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          creds: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CREDENTIALS }}

      - name: Install Bicep
        run: az bicep install

      - name: Build ARM JSON
        run: |
          az bicep build \
            --file managed-app/main.bicep \
            --outfile managed-app/mainTemplate.json

      - name: Package
        run: |
          VERSION=${GITHUB_SHA::8}
          zip -j managed-app-${VERSION}.zip \
            managed-app/mainTemplate.json \
            managed-app/createUiDefinition.json
          echo "BLOB=managed-app-${VERSION}.zip" >> $GITHUB_ENV
          echo "VERSION=${VERSION}" >> $GITHUB_ENV

      - name: Upload to Storage
        run: |
          az storage blob upload \
            --account-name $STORAGE_ACCOUNT \
            --container-name $STORAGE_CONTAINER \
            --name $BLOB --file $BLOB
          SAS=$(az storage blob generate-sas \
            --account-name $STORAGE_ACCOUNT \
            --container-name $STORAGE_CONTAINER \
            --name $BLOB --permissions r \
            --expiry 2026-12-31 --full-uri -o tsv)
          echo "SAS_URI=$SAS" >> $GITHUB_ENV

      - name: Update Definition
        run: |
          MGMT_ID=$(az ad group show --group PlatformOps --query id -o tsv)
          az managedapp definition create \
            --name $DEFINITION_NAME \
            --resource-group $DEFINITION_RG \
            --location eastus \
            --lock-level ReadOnly \
            --display-name "Standard Web App ${VERSION}" \
            --authorizations "${MGMT_ID}:b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c" \
            --package-file-uri "$SAS_URI" \
            --tags version=$VERSION commit=$GITHUB_SHA
Use semantic versioning for blob names: managed-app-1.0.0.zip, managed-app-1.1.0.zip, etc.
Keep old definition resources (e.g. StandardWebApp-v1, StandardWebApp-v2) so consumers on older versions continue working.
Tag definitions with the version and commit SHA for traceability: --tags version=1.1.0 commit=abc123.
When breaking changes are required (parameter additions or removals), create a new definition with a new name and communicate the upgrade path to consumers.
For Marketplace offers, use Partner Center plan versions — increment the plan version number rather than updating the same version in-place, as in-place updates affect all existing deployments.
Before creating the application definition, test the full deployment flow in a sandbox subscription to ensure the template deploys successfully and the portal wizard renders correctly.

# 1. Deploy the template directly to confirm it works
az group create --name test-managed-rg --location eastus

az deployment group create \
  --resource-group test-managed-rg \
  --template-file mainTemplate.json \
  --parameters appName=testapp appServiceSku=B1

# 2. Deploy via the managed app mechanism in a test definition
az managedapp definition create \
  --name TestApp \
  --resource-group test-definitions-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --lock-level None \
  --display-name 'Test App (staging)' \
  --authorizations "$(az ad signed-in-user show --query id -o tsv):8e3af657-a8ff-443c-a75c-2fe8c4bcb635" \
  --package-file-uri "$SAS_URI"

# 3. Deploy the test managed app
az managedapp create \
  --name testapp \
  --resource-group consumer-test-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --kind ServiceCatalog \
  --managed-rg-id "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)/resourceGroups/testapp-mrg" \
  --managedapp-definition-id "$(az managedapp definition show --name TestApp --resource-group test-definitions-rg --query id -o tsv)" \
  --parameters '{"appName": {"value": "testapp"}, "appServiceSku": {"value": "B1"}}'

# 4. Clean up test resources
az group delete --name testapp-mrg --yes --no-wait
az group delete --name consumer-test-rg --yes --no-wait
Set --lock-level None for test definitions. This lets you clean up the managed resource group directly without hitting delete-prevention locks during the testing cycle.
The createUiDefinition supports over 20 element types for building rich portal wizards. The table below covers the most frequently used elements.
Single-line text input with regex validation
App name, DNS prefix, email address
Groups elements under a collapsible header
VNet + subnet picker from existing resources

A single application definition is scoped to a resource group and inherits that resource group's location. The definition metadata is stored in that region, but consumers can deploy the managed application (and its managed resource group) in any region where the template resources are available.

# Create definition in West Europe for EU consumers
az managedapp definition create \
  --name StandardWebApp \
  --resource-group platform-westeurope-rg \
  --location westeurope \
  --lock-level ReadOnly \
  --display-name 'Standard Web App (EU)' \
  --authorizations "${MGMT_GROUP_ID}:${CONTRIBUTOR_ROLE}" \
  --package-file-uri "$SAS_URI"

# List definitions across all regions
az managedapp definition list \
  --query '[].{name:name,location:location,rg:resourceGroup}' \
  -o table 2>/dev/null || \
for RG in $(az group list --query '[].name' -o tsv); do
  az managedapp definition list --resource-group $RG -o table 2>/dev/null
done

For global organizations, publish definitions in at least two regions for availability. If the definition's resource group is unavailable, consumers in that region cannot discover the definition — but existing deployments are unaffected, as they are independent resources.

When a new version of the package is ready, update the definition by re-running az managedapp definition create with the same --name. This is an idempotent upsert — existing consumer deployments are not affected until they explicitly redeploy or the publisher pushes an update to the managed resource group.

# Build and upload new version
az bicep build --file main.bicep --outfile mainTemplate.json
zip -j managed-app-v1.1.0.zip mainTemplate.json createUiDefinition.json

az storage blob upload \
  --account-name mytemplatesstorage \
  --container-name managed-apps \
  --name managed-app-v1.1.0.zip \
  --file managed-app-v1.1.0.zip

NEW_URI=$(az storage blob generate-sas \
  --account-name mytemplatesstorage \
  --container-name managed-apps \
  --name managed-app-v1.1.0.zip \
  --permissions r --expiry 2026-12-31 --full-uri -o tsv)

# Update the definition (upsert)
az managedapp definition create \
  --name StandardWebApp \
  --resource-group platform-rg \
  --location eastus \
  --lock-level ReadOnly \
  --display-name 'Standard Web App v1.1' \
  --description 'Added Key Vault integration' \
  --authorizations "${MGMT_GROUP_ID}:${CONTRIBUTOR_ROLE}" \
  --package-file-uri "$NEW_URI" \
  --tags version=1.1.0

# Verify update
az managedapp definition show \
  --name StandardWebApp --resource-group platform-rg \
  --query '{displayName:properties.displayName,tags:tags}'

Updating the definition does not automatically redeploy to existing consumer managed resource groups. Consumers who deployed from the old definition continue running the old template. To push updates to existing deployments, deploy the new mainTemplate.json directly into each consumer's managed resource group using your management access.

Publishing a managed application follows five deterministic steps: author (mainTemplate.json + createUiDefinition.json), validate (arm-ttk + UI sandbox + az deployment group validate), package (zip -j), upload (blob storage + SAS URI), and publish (az managedapp definition create). Automating this with a CI/CD pipeline ensures every merge to main produces a versioned, tested definition. Versioning strategy — keeping old definitions active while publishing new ones — ensures consumers on older versions are not disrupted by publisher upgrades.
